title: useful functions
body:
A PPENDIX B Useful Functions, Integrals and Sums

Collected here are some calculations and formulas that are espeically pertinent in probability modeling. We begin with several exponential integrals, the first and simplest being Z e−x dx = −e−x . (B.1)

When we use integration by parts, the second integral that we introduce reduces to the first in the manner Z Z xe dx = −xe + e−x dx = −e−x (1 + x). −x −x

These are the special cases α = 1 and α = 2, respectively, in the general formula valid for any real number α for which the integrals are defined: Z Z x e dx = −x e + (α − 1) xα−2 e−x dx. α−1 −x α−1 −x (B.2)

Fixing the limits of integration leads to the Gamma function, defined by Z ∞ Γ(α) = xα−1 e−x dx, for α > 0.

From (B.2), it follows that Γ(α) = (α − 1) × Γ(α − 1), and therefore, for any integer k,

Γ(k) = (k − 1) × (k − 2) × × 2 × Γ(1).

A direct consequence of (B.1) is that Γ(1) = 1, which with the above shows that the Gamma function at integral arguments is a generalization of the factorial:

Γ(k) = (k − 1)!, for k = 1, 2, 3,

A more difficult integration shows that

√   Γ = π.

Stirling’s formula is another important asymptotic evaluation of the factorial function:

n! = nn e−n (2πn)1/2 er(n)/12n ,  42 Appendix B. Useful Functions, Integrals and Sums

whereby 1− < r(n) < 1. 12n + 1 This is often written in the looser form

n! ∼ nn e−n (2πn)1/2 as n → ∞, (B.3)

the symbol “∼” signifying that the two quantities are asymptotically equivalent (their ratio approaches 1 as n → ∞). For the binomial coefficient   n n! = , k (n − k)!k!

For n = 1, 2,, Newton’s binomial expansion provides the evaluation n   n X n k k (1 − x) = (−1) x , for − ∞ < x < ∞. k=0 k

The formula may be generalized for nonintegral n by appropriately generalizing the binomial coefficient, defining for any real number α,     α(α − 1) (α − k + 1) α for k = 1, 2, = k! k  1 for k = 0.

The general binomial theorem, valid for all real α, is ∞   α X α k k (1 − x) = (−1) x , for − 1 < x < 1. (B.5) k=0 k

When α = −n for a positive integer n, we obtain a group of formulas useful in dealing with geometric series. For a positive integer n, in view of (B.4) and (B.5), we have ∞   X n+k−1 (1 − x) −n = xk , for |x| < 1. k=0 k

The familiar formula ∞ X 1 xk = for |x| < 1 k=0 1−x for the sum of a geometric series results from the foregoing with n = 1. The cases n = 2 and n = 3 yield ∞ X 1 (k + 1)xk = for |x| < 1 k=0 (1 − x)2 and ∞ X 2 (k + 2)(k + 1)xk = for |x| < 1. k=0 (1 − x)3 Finally, let us mention the following sums of powers of integers: n X n(n + 1) k= k=1 n X n(n + 1)(2n + 1) k2 = k=1 n X n2 (n + 1)2 k3 = . k=1
